threats.title: onlyOperator Modifier Inverted Logic Locks Out All Designated Operators
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyOperator modifier uses `if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert` which reverts when operator[msg.sender] is true. This means designated operators can NEVER call harvest, delegateAmount, undelegateAmount, redelegateAmount, harvestAndDelegateRewards, or finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch. Additionally, if the owner is also set as an operator via updateOperator(owner, true), even the owner is permanently locked out of all operator functions until the mapping is toggled back.
threats.investigation_rationale: The boolean condition `msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]` evaluates to true (reverts) whenever operator[msg.sender] is true, regardless of ownership. The intended logic is clearly `msg.sender != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]` — allowing either the owner OR an operator to proceed. This is a critical access control defect affecting all staking management functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert — OR operator should use AND with negation: != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]

preconditions: An address is set as operator via updateOperator(addr, true)
That operator attempts to call any onlyOperator-guarded function

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect boolean logic in access control modifier
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: operator[msg.sender] being true causes revert instead of allowing access
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No test coverage for operator-as-non-owner calling gated functions

analysis_questions: Does the deployment script set any operators? (No — only owner deploys and calls directly)
Can the owner still call operator functions? (Yes, only if operator[owner] is false)
If owner calls updateOperator(owner, true), are all operator functions bricked?
validation_requirements: Test: set operator[addr]=true, have addr call harvest() — expect revert
Test: set operator[owner]=true, have owner call delegateAmount() — expect revert
Verify no alternate code path bypasses the modifier

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After deployment, if the owner sets a bot as operator and assumes the bot handles harvesting/finalization, all staking management stops. User withdrawal requests via requestWithdrawal cannot be finalized. The owner must directly manage all operations without being flagged as operator.
prerequisites: Owner sets at least one operator address via updateOperator

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total protocol paralysis if owner is also set as operator; delegation of staking ops impossible otherwise
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users — staking cannot be managed by designated operators
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can toggle operator[owner]=false to regain access, but designated operators remain permanently locked out

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying that non-owner operators can call operator-gated functions

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Immediate on any operator function call

threats.title: LiquidProxy.harvest() Claims Rewards N Times Redundantly, Risking DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidProxy.harvest() loops over _consensusAddrs.length and calls claimRewards(_consensusAddrs) — passing the FULL array — on EACH iteration. This means rewards are claimed N times for the same set of validators. The first call claims all rewards; subsequent calls attempt to claim zero. If the Ronin staking contract reverts on zero-reward claims, harvest becomes impossible for arrays with length > 1. Additionally, the function uses address(this).balance as claimed amount, which includes any pre-existing native RON from prior operations or external sends to the proxy's open receive().
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop iterates `_consensusAddrs.length` times, but each iteration passes the entire `_consensusAddrs` array to claimRewards, not just `_consensusAddrs[i]`. This is a clear code defect — the intent was likely to call once or to call per-address.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (i = 0; i < _consensusAddrs.length; i++) { claimRewards(_consensusAddrs); } — passes full array in loop

preconditions: _consensusAddrs.length > 1
Ronin staking contract behavior on duplicate/zero-reward claims must be verified

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Full array passed in loop instead of single element or single call
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: N redundant claimRewards calls; also address(this).balance includes pre-existing RON
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No balance delta tracking; no single-call pattern

analysis_questions: Does Ronin staking contract revert on claimRewards with zero claimable rewards?
If so, harvest is permanently DoS'd for multi-validator arrays
How much gas is wasted on redundant calls?
validation_requirements: Test claimRewards behavior with zero rewards (does it revert?)
Measure gas cost with multiple validators
Test harvest with pre-existing RON in proxy via open receive()

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If Ronin staking reverts on zero-reward claims, any harvest call with >1 consensus address fails after the first iteration claims everything. This blocks all reward collection. Even if it doesn't revert, N-1 redundant calls waste gas and the balance-based accounting is inaccurate.
prerequisites: Multiple consensus addresses passed to harvest
Ronin staking contract behavior on duplicate claims

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete reward harvesting failure if staking contract reverts on empty claims; otherwise gas waste and accounting inaccuracy
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users — rewards cannot be harvested
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Workaround: call harvest with single-element arrays (wastes gas)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for multi-validator harvest behavior

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily detectable via failed harvest transactions

threats.title: No Withdrawal Request Cancellation Creates Permanent Fund Lock Risk
threats.hypothesis_description: Users who call requestWithdrawal() transfer their LRON shares to the vault contract. There is no mechanism to cancel a withdrawal request and reclaim shares. If finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() is never called for the current epoch (due to operator unavailability, the onlyOperator bug INV-ACCESS-001, or contract pause), user shares are permanently locked in the vault with no recovery path. Combined with the onlyOperator bug, if the owner is also flagged as operator, ALL withdrawal requests become permanently irrecoverable.
threats.investigation_rationale: requestWithdrawal transfers shares via _transfer(msg.sender, address(this), _shares). The only path to recover value is through finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch (burns shares, sends WRON to escrow) followed by redeem(epoch). There is no cancelWithdrawal function to return shares to the user.

proof_of_signal.pattern: shares transferred to vault via _transfer with no cancel/recovery mechanism; finalization is sole exit path

preconditions: User calls requestWithdrawal with non-zero shares
finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch is not called for the current epoch

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: One-way transfer with no cancellation mechanism
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: User loses control of shares with only operator-dependent recovery
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No cancelWithdrawal function; no timeout-based auto-return

analysis_questions: Is there any alternate path for the vault to return locked shares?
Could the owner use a different function to process locked shares?
What is the maximum duration funds could be locked?
validation_requirements: Confirm no alternate recovery path exists in the codebase
Test scenario: requestWithdrawal followed by indefinite finalization delay
Assess interaction with onlyOperator bug

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users request withdrawal during normal operation. The operator (or owner) becomes unavailable or the onlyOperator bug prevents finalization. All shares in the current epoch's locked pool are permanently inaccessible. Users cannot withdraw, redeem, or transfer those shares.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of all shares submitted via requestWithdrawal for the unfinalized epoch
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who called requestWithdrawal for the affected epoch

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for withdrawal cancellation or recovery from failed finalization

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Locked shares visible on-chain in vault balance

threats.title: redeem(uint256 _epoch) Emits Wrong Epoch in WithdrawalClaimed Event
threats.hypothesis_description: In the redeem(uint256 _epoch) function, a local variable `uint256 epoch = withdrawalEpoch` shadows the parameter _epoch. The WithdrawalClaimed event emits `epoch` (current global withdrawalEpoch) instead of `_epoch` (the actual epoch being redeemed). This causes all event emissions to reference the current epoch rather than the claimed epoch.
threats.investigation_rationale: Line `uint256 epoch = withdrawalEpoch;` creates a local variable used in the event. The event should reference _epoch. This is a clear variable naming bug.

proof_of_signal.pattern: emit WithdrawalClaimed(msg.sender, epoch, shares, assets) — epoch is withdrawalEpoch, not _epoch

preconditions: User calls redeem for a finalized epoch
withdrawalEpoch has advanced past the epoch being redeemed

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Local variable shadows function parameter in event emission
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Event data is incorrect — off-chain indexers track wrong epoch
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No compiler warning on unused _epoch in event context

analysis_questions: Do any off-chain systems depend on the epoch field in WithdrawalClaimed?
Could this cause accounting errors in protocol analytics?
validation_requirements: Verify event emission with a test: redeem epoch 0 when withdrawalEpoch is 5

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User redeems epoch 2 when current withdrawalEpoch is 5. Event emits epoch 5 instead of epoch 2. Off-chain indexers incorrectly attribute the claim to epoch 5.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Off-chain systems, dashboards, analytics relying on event data

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying correct epoch in WithdrawalClaimed event

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare event epoch vs function parameter in transaction traces

threats.title: Permissionless pruneValidatorList Can Deflate totalAssets During Transient States
threats.hypothesis_description: pruneValidatorList() is callable by any address and removes validators that show zero rewards and zero staking across all proxies. If the Ronin staking contract has transient states (e.g., after undelegation but before cooldown completion, or during block-level state transitions), a validator could temporarily show 0 for staking amounts despite having active stake. Pruning during this window removes the validator from the tracking list, causing getTotalStaked() and getTotalRewards() to exclude their stake/rewards from totalAssets(), deflating share price.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function queries point-in-time values from the external staking contract. If these values are temporarily zero (due to staking contract internal state transitions), the validator is incorrectly pruned. The pruned validator's stake is no longer counted in totalAssets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No access control; point-in-time staking checks may miss transient non-zero states

preconditions: Validator has active stake but Ronin staking contract returns 0 during transient state
Attacker calls pruneValidatorList during this window

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Permissionless function with external state dependency
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Staking contract may have transient zero-balance states during undelegation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No access control, no cooldown, no operator confirmation

analysis_questions: Does Ronin staking contract have cooldown periods where staking amounts read as 0?
Can undelegation create a window where getManyStakingAmounts returns 0?
What is the share price impact of pruning an active validator?
validation_requirements: Test pruneValidatorList timing relative to undelegation
Query Ronin staking contract during various state transitions
Measure totalAssets before and after incorrect pruning

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker observes an undelegateAmount call on-chain. In a subsequent block, they call pruneValidatorList. If the staking contract shows 0 for the undelegated validator (cooldown in progress), the validator is pruned. totalAssets drops by the staked amount. Attacker deposits at deflated price, waits for validator to be re-added via next delegation, redeems at restored price.
prerequisites: Ronin staking has transient zero-balance states
Validator has active but temporarily invisible stake

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Share price manipulation proportional to pruned validator's stake
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All holders experience temporary share price deflation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Validator re-added on next delegation; temporary loss for those who withdrew

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for pruning during transient staking states

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Capital for deposit at deflated price
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block: observe undelegation, wait for transient state, prune, deposit

threats.title: ERC4626 First Depositor Inflation Attack Due to Zero _decimalsOffset
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidRon does not override _decimalsOffset() (defaults to 0), providing minimal virtual share/asset offset (10^0 = 1). An attacker can be the first depositor with 1 wei, mint 1 share, then donate a large WRON amount directly to the vault. This inflates totalAssets so that subsequent depositors' previewDeposit returns 0 shares for substantial deposits. The attacker then redeems their 1 share for the entire vault balance including the victim's deposit.
threats.investigation_rationale: OpenZeppelin's ERC4626 uses _decimalsOffset to create virtual shares/assets that resist inflation. With offset=0, the virtual shares are just +1, providing negligible protection. The classic first-depositor attack becomes viable.
deployment_assumptions: Deployment script deposits 10000 ether as first deposit, effectively mitigating this attack in the expected deployment
threats.config_evidence: LiquidRon_saigon.s.sol: liquidRon.deposit{value: 10000 ether}() — initial large deposit provides protection. However, the code itself has no minimum deposit or dead shares mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No _decimalsOffset override; inherits default 0 from OpenZeppelin ERC4626

preconditions: Vault has zero or near-zero total supply (no initial seed deposit)
Attacker is the first depositor
WRON can be transferred directly to the vault address

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LiquidRon.sol — no _decimalsOffset override visible in contract
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: 1-share inflation attack feasible with ~10 WRON donation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum deposit, no dead shares, no _decimalsOffset override

analysis_questions: Does the deployment always include a large initial deposit?
Could the vault reach zero supply after deployment (all shares redeemed)?
What is the minimum donation needed to steal next deposit?
validation_requirements: Verify OpenZeppelin version confirms _decimalsOffset defaults to 0
Simulate: deposit 1 wei, donate 10 WRON, check next depositor's shares
Assess if vault can reach zero supply in production

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If deployed without initial seed deposit, or if all shares are later redeemed: attacker deposits 1 wei (1 share), transfers 10 WRON to vault, victim deposits 9.99 WRON → gets 0 shares. Attacker redeems 1 share for ~20 WRON.
prerequisites: Attacker is first depositor or total supply returns to ~0

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Near-total theft of subsequent depositor's funds

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for first depositor attack scenario

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Small WRON donation (~10 WRON to steal ~10 WRON from victim)
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Must be first depositor or when total supply is near zero
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Large donation to vault visible on-chain

threats.title: _checkUserCanReceiveRon Uses Zero-Value Check That Doesn't Guarantee Non-Zero Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: _checkUserCanReceiveRon sends a 0-value call to verify the user can receive RON. However, many contracts accept 0-value calls (2300 gas stipend sufficient) but revert on non-zero value transfers (e.g., due to gas limits in receive/fallback or conditional logic). A user contract could pass this check during requestWithdrawal but later fail to receive RON in the redeem(epoch) call, permanently locking their withdrawal claim — the request is marked fulfilled but the assets can never be sent.
threats.investigation_rationale: The check `payable(_user).call{value: 0}('')` succeeds for almost all addresses but doesn't validate ability to receive actual value. When redeem(epoch) calls _withdrawRONTo which sends non-zero RON, the call may fail with ErrWithdrawFailed, but request.fulfilled is NOT yet set (wait — checking the code: fulfilled IS set before the transfer). Actually: request.fulfilled = true is set BEFORE _withdrawRONTo. So if _withdrawRONTo reverts, the entire transaction reverts and fulfilled stays false. User can retry. But if the user contract never accepts non-zero RON, claims are permanently stuck.

proof_of_signal.pattern: payable(_user).call{value: 0}('') — 0-value check doesn't prove non-zero value acceptance

preconditions: User is a contract that accepts 0-value calls but reverts on non-zero value
User calls requestWithdrawal (passes check)
User later calls redeem (reverts on _withdrawRONTo)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: 0-value call check is insufficient to validate non-zero RON receipt
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Contract users may pass check but fail actual withdrawal
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum value check; no WRON fallback option

analysis_questions: Are there common contract patterns that accept 0-value but reject non-zero?
Does the revert in _withdrawRONTo properly roll back fulfilled status?
Is there an alternative path for contract users to claim WRON instead of RON?
validation_requirements: Deploy contract that accepts 0-value but reverts on non-zero, test requestWithdrawal + redeem flow
Verify transaction reverts properly (no partial state changes)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A smart contract wallet passes _checkUserCanReceiveRon during requestWithdrawal. Shares are locked in the epoch. After finalization, the contract calls redeem(epoch). _withdrawRONTo sends non-zero RON which the contract cannot accept. The call reverts. The user's shares remain locked — they can keep retrying, but if the contract can never accept RON, the funds are permanently stuck.
prerequisites: User is a contract with restricted receive/fallback

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: User's withdrawal claim is permanently unredeemable if they can never accept native RON
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Contract-based users with restricted receive functionality
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None if the user contract cannot be upgraded

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test with contracts that accept 0-value but reject non-zero value

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: 2300 gas stipend vs actual transfer gas requirements
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Repeated failed redeem transactions from specific addresses

threats.title: Undelegation path assumes exact/immediate RON return; mismatch can revert or silently mis-account, breaking staking/vault fund conservation
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidProxy.undelegateAmount computes `totalUndelegated` as the sum of requested amounts and immediately wraps+sends exactly that amount to the vault. If Ronin staking’s `bulkUndelegate` has a cooldown, returns less due to slashing/rounding, or returns asynchronously, the proxy may not have enough native RON to wrap, causing a revert; or it may wrap more than newly received by using residual RON on the proxy, distorting accounting.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fragile integration assumption with practical likelihood in staking protocols (cooldowns and partial returns are common). If triggered, it can halt undelegations and prevent rebalancing or withdrawal funding.
deployment_assumptions: Ronin staking bulkUndelegate does not synchronously transfer exact requested RON to caller in the same transaction (cooldown/partial return/slash)
threats.config_evidence: Repo contains only interface; no Ronin staking implementation included. Must verify actual behavior at configured roninStaking address.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalUndelegated=sum(_amounts); bulkUndelegate(...); _depositRONTo(vault, totalUndelegated) without checking actual balance delta

preconditions: Operator/vault calls undelegateAmount with amounts > 0
Ronin staking does not transfer exact amount immediately OR any slashing/rounding occurs
Proxy has insufficient native balance to cover totalUndelegated at wrap time

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Assuming external protocol returns exact funds immediately; no balance-delta reconciliation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Revert on wrap due to insufficient native balance; or mis-account by using residual proxy balance
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check of address(this).balance before/after bulkUndelegate; no asynchronous claim/withdraw function

analysis_questions: Does Ronin staking bulkUndelegate pay out immediately to msg.sender? Is there a waiting period?
Can undelegated amount differ from requested amounts (slashing/rounding)?
Can proxy have residual native RON that would be mixed into accounting?
validation_requirements: Fork test: call bulkUndelegate and inspect proxy balance delta
Simulate partial return (mock staking) and confirm revert in _depositRONTo
Add invariant tests around proxy balance changes and WRON transferred to vault

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If Ronin staking introduces/has cooldowns, any undelegate attempt reverts because proxy tries to wrap unavailable RON. This blocks operators from making funds liquid, potentially preventing withdrawals and rebalancing.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential withdrawal unavailability (liquidity deadlock) and operational failure of staking management
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users needing liquidity during stressed conditions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May require new flow (claim later) and redeploy/migration

threats.test_coverage_gap: No integration tests against Ronin staking undelegation semantics

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: single transaction; or multi-step if protocol uses cooldown
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor undelegate tx reverts and proxy native balance behavior

threats.title: Withdrawal-request redemption uses custom _convertToAssets (with +1/+offset) differing from ERC4626 conversion used at epoch finalization; rounding mismatch may over/under-pay claimants
threats.hypothesis_description: Epoch finalization sets `assetSupply = previewRedeem(lockedShares)` using OZ ERC4626 math, but later claim uses a custom `_convertToAssets(shares, lock.assetSupply, lock.shareSupply)` with `(_totalAssets + 1)` and `(_totalShares + 10**_decimalsOffset())`. This mismatch can create systematic rounding differences, potentially enabling some users to redeem slightly more/less than intended across many requests, and could break the invariant that total claimed per epoch <= assetSupply.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even small rounding bugs can accumulate or be gamed by splitting requests into many small claims if claim calculation favors the claimant. The divergence from standard ERC4626 conversions is a concrete signal worth validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _convertToAssets uses mulDiv((_totalAssets+1), (_totalShares+10**_decimalsOffset()), Floor) differing from OZ ERC4626 previewRedeem used at finalization

preconditions: At least one epoch finalized with lockedPricePerSharePerEpoch set
Multiple users redeem from the same epoch OR a user can create multiple small redemption-eligible positions via requestWithdrawal splits
Rounding direction benefits claimant in some cases (to be tested)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Inconsistent conversion formulas between settlement and claim
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Possibility of aggregate claims exceeding the escrow-funded assetSupply or leaving dust that is unclaimable
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No per-epoch claimedAssets accounting to enforce cap; relies on math correctness alone

analysis_questions: Does custom _convertToAssets exactly match OZ ERC4626 conversion at settlement for the stored (assetSupply, shareSupply)?
Can a user increase payout by splitting shares into multiple requestWithdrawal entries (same epoch) or via rounding artifacts?
Does sum of user claims ever exceed lockLog.assetSupply due to rounding-up effects?
validation_requirements: Property test: for a finalized epoch, sum(convert(request.shares)) across users <= assetSupply
Compare lockLog.assetSupply vs sum of user redemptions under adversarial share splits
Check behavior for small share amounts and very large totals

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker requests withdrawal in a pattern that maximizes rounding gains at claim-time under the custom formula (e.g., splitting into many small share amounts if it increases floor rounding outcomes). Over many epochs/claims, attacker extracts extra WRON from escrow relative to their fair share, harming other claimants.
prerequisites: Sufficient ability to split shares and create many claim entries (requestWithdrawal accumulates per user per epoch, so split benefit must come from other dimensions—e.g., multiple accounts)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate: rounding-based leakage or underpayment; could cause escrow shortfall for late claimants in worst case
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Epoch claimants; especially late claimants if escrow depleted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May require recalculation/migration; could potentially be patched with cap accounting

threats.test_coverage_gap: No invariant tests ensuring per-epoch aggregate redemption never exceeds locked assetSupply

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Track per-epoch escrow balance vs expected remaining liabilities; identify drift

threats.title: harvestAndDelegateRewards Creates Unbacked Operator Fee Draining User Deposits
threats.hypothesis_description: When harvestAndDelegateRewards is called, staking rewards are re-delegated to a validator via delegateRewards (RON never enters the vault). However, operatorFeeAmount is incremented by (claimableAmount * operatorFee / BIPS). This fee is entirely unbacked by WRON in the vault. When feeRecipient calls fetchOperatorFee(), the vault's _withdrawRONTo unwraps and sends WRON from the vault balance — which consists of user deposits — to the fee recipient. This is a direct drain of depositor principal.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental accounting violation: fee obligation is created without corresponding asset inflow. Repeated harvestAndDelegateRewards calls compound the unbacked liability. fetchOperatorFee is the trigger that materializes the loss from user deposits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: operatorFeeAmount += (harvestedAmount * operatorFee) / BIPS but LiquidProxy.harvestAndDelegateRewards calls delegateRewards which re-stakes rewards without sending any WRON to vault

preconditions: Owner calls harvestAndDelegateRewards (operator function works for owner not flagged as operator)
operatorFee > 0 (deployment sets 250 = 2.5%)
Validators have claimable rewards
feeRecipient calls fetchOperatorFee

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: LiquidProxy.harvestAndDelegateRewards returns claimableAmount from delegateRewards (re-staked, no RON enters vault). LiquidRon increments operatorFeeAmount by claimableAmount * 250 / 10000
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Normal protocol operation: repeated harvestAndDelegateRewards accumulates unbacked fee debt against user deposits
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Fee recipient extracts (total_rewards_compounded * 2.5%) from user deposits. Over time, this could be substantial.

economic_questions: What is the expected reward rate per epoch and cumulative unbacked fee amount?
Can operatorFeeAmount exceed vault WRON balance, causing fetchOperatorFee to revert?
How does this interact with totalAssets inflation (INV-ECON-002)?
validation_requirements: Trace full flow: harvestAndDelegateRewards → verify vault WRON balance unchanged → fetchOperatorFee → verify WRON drained from deposits
Calculate cumulative drain under realistic compounding scenarios
Test: after N harvestAndDelegateRewards calls, verify operatorFeeAmount > vault WRON from deposits

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 2.5% of all compounded rewards, extracted from depositor principal

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Continuous — each harvestAndDelegateRewards call increases the drain
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Slow but continuous drain of depositor principal; share price deflates when fee is claimed

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional share of total unbacked fee amount
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: 2.5% of all rewards compounded via harvestAndDelegateRewards, subtracted from user deposits

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: operatorFee=250, BIPS=10000 → 2.5% of compounded rewards drained from deposits
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare vault WRON balance before/after harvestAndDelegateRewards and fetchOperatorFee

threats.title: totalAssets Inflation by Unreduced operatorFeeAmount Enables Share Price Arbitrage
threats.hypothesis_description: After harvest() is called, the full gross reward amount enters the vault as WRON (counted in super.totalAssets()). The operatorFeeAmount is tracked separately but NOT subtracted from totalAssets(). Before harvest, getTotalRewards() deducts the fee portion from unrealized rewards. After harvest, rewards are realized as WRON in vault balance but the fee portion is no longer deducted anywhere. This creates a window where totalAssets() is inflated by operatorFeeAmount, increasing share price. An attacker holding shares can redeem at the inflated price after observing harvest on-chain, then re-deposit after fetchOperatorFee deflates the price.
threats.investigation_rationale: The inflation equals (harvestedAmount * operatorFee / BIPS) and persists until fetchOperatorFee is called. On Ronin (no public mempool), the attacker observes confirmed harvest transactions on-chain and acts in subsequent blocks — no front-running required.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalAssets() = super.totalAssets() + getTotalStaked() + getTotalRewards() — missing `- operatorFeeAmount` deduction from vault WRON balance

preconditions: harvest() has been called (rewards realized as WRON in vault)
operatorFeeAmount > 0
fetchOperatorFee has not yet been called
Attacker holds LRON shares or can obtain them

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Before harvest: totalAssets includes (rewards - fee_portion) via getTotalRewards(). After harvest: totalAssets includes full rewards as WRON, fee_portion not deducted → net inflation = fee_portion
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker monitors on-chain harvest events, redeems shares at inflated price, re-deposits after fetchOperatorFee

economic_questions: What is the typical time window between harvest and fetchOperatorFee?
What is the expected reward amount per harvest cycle?
Is the profit meaningful after gas costs on Ronin?
validation_requirements: Calculate totalAssets before/after harvest with concrete numbers
Simulate redeem at inflated price → fetchOperatorFee → re-deposit cycle
Measure share price delta and attacker profit per cycle

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Proportional to desired extraction — larger share holding = larger profit
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.02 ETH gas for redeem + deposit on Ronin
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: For 100 ETH harvest, inflation = 2.5 ETH. 10% share holder extracts ~0.25 ETH per cycle
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on share fraction and harvest size
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-block: observe harvest, redeem, wait for fee claim, re-deposit

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after harvest() confirmation, before fetchOperatorFee()
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Each cycle extracts value from remaining depositors proportional to fee inflation

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: operatorFee=250, typical harvest amounts on Ronin validators
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare totalAssets pre/post harvest, quantify inflation window

threats.title: LiquidProxy.harvest() Captures Unrelated Native RON as Rewards via address(this).balance
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidProxy.harvest() uses `uint256 claimedAmount = address(this).balance` after calling claimRewards, treating the proxy's entire native RON balance as harvested rewards. Since the proxy has an open receive() function, anyone can send native RON to the proxy. This extra RON is then: (1) wrapped and sent to the vault, (2) has operatorFeeAmount calculated on it (fee applied to non-reward funds), and (3) inflates the harvest amount. Additionally, residual RON from previous operations (e.g., bulkUndelegate returning RON to proxy before _depositRONTo) would also be miscounted.
threats.investigation_rationale: The accounting error means harvested amount ≠ actual staking rewards. Any pre-existing native RON in the proxy inflates operatorFeeAmount and distorts totalAssets. The open receive() makes this externally triggerable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `uint256 claimedAmount = address(this).balance` captures entire native RON balance, not just the delta from claimRewards; open `receive() external payable {}` allows external deposits

preconditions: LiquidProxy has pre-existing native RON (from donation or residual operations)
Owner calls harvest() on that proxy

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: claimedAmount = address(this).balance (includes all native RON, not just rewards). operatorFeeAmount += (claimedAmount * operatorFee) / BIPS charges fee on non-reward funds
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Anyone can send RON to proxy via receive(). This RON is captured on next harvest and fee is applied.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Increases operatorFeeAmount incorrectly, exacerbating the INV-ECON-001 and INV-ECON-002 violations. Combined with harvestAndDelegateRewards phantom fee or totalAssets inflation.

economic_questions: Can residual RON accumulate in proxy from normal operations (e.g., partial undelegation refunds)?
How much RON donation would meaningfully distort operatorFeeAmount?
Does the claimRewards redundant loop (N calls) create any residual state?
validation_requirements: Send RON to proxy, then call harvest, verify claimedAmount includes donation
Calculate fee overcollection on donated RON
Check if undelegateAmount or other flows leave residual native RON in proxy

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Variable — donation amount is attacker's choice
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas to send RON to proxy (~0.001 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: inflates operatorFeeAmount which drains depositors when fetched. 2.5% of donated RON is additional drain
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Net negative for external attacker (donation > fee extraction). Profitable if attacker IS fee recipient.

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Accounting distortion proportional to donation size

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to donation relative to TVL
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: 2.5% of any non-reward RON captured by harvest is additional drain on deposits

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check proxy balance before/after normal operations for residual RON
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Balance-delta accounting vs full-balance accounting comparison

threats.title: WRON.transfer() in RonHelper Does Not Check Return Value
threats.hypothesis_description: RonHelper._depositRONTo() calls `IWRON(wron).transfer(to, amount)` without checking the return value. The IWRON interface declares transfer with no return value. If the WRON contract returns false on failure instead of reverting (as some ERC20 implementations do), the transfer would silently fail. This would mean WRON deposits, harvests, and undelegations could complete without actually moving tokens, creating phantom balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: Standard WETH implementations revert on failure, but the code assumes this behavior without verification. If WRON has any non-standard behavior (e.g., returning false), critical fund transfers would silently fail across all deposit and withdrawal flows.
deployment_assumptions: WRON token reverts on transfer failure (WETH-like behavior)
threats.config_evidence: WRON address 0xA959726154953bAe111746E265E6d754F48570E6 on Saigon testnet; standard WETH behavior assumed but not verified in code

proof_of_signal.pattern: `IWRON(wron).transfer(to, amount)` — raw transfer call without return value check or SafeERC20

preconditions: WRON token returns false instead of reverting on transfer failure
Any function calling _depositRONTo is invoked

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: IWRON interface declares `function transfer(address, uint256) external` with no return type. Raw call ignores any return value.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If transfer fails silently, WRON stays in vault/proxy but accounting proceeds as if transferred
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Phantom transfers could desynchronize WRON balances across vault, escrow, and proxies

economic_questions: Does the Ronin WRON contract revert on transfer failure or return false?
What scenarios could cause a WRON transfer to fail (insufficient balance, blocked address)?
What is the blast radius if a _depositRONTo transfer silently fails?
validation_requirements: Verify WRON contract source code on Ronin for transfer behavior
Test transfer with insufficient balance
Consider using SafeERC20.safeTransfer or checking return value

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on which flow has silent failure

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Potential desynchronization of all WRON balances if WRON is non-standard

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users if WRON transfers fail silently
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Variable — depends on failed transfer context

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: WRON contract source code / behavior verification
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare IWRON interface vs actual WRON ABI

threats.title: Withdrawal Request Starvation via Opportunistic Liquid Withdrawals
threats.hypothesis_description: When the vault lacks sufficient liquid WRON to finalize an epoch, the operator must undelegate stake. However, once the undelegated RON enters the vault, it is immediately available to any user calling the standard ERC4626 `withdraw()` function. Opportunistic users can instantly drain the newly available liquid WRON before the operator can call `finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch()`. This creates a starvation condition where users who properly used `requestWithdrawal()` can never have their epochs finalized.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the withdrawal queue mechanism, allowing liquid withdrawers to front-cut queued users indefinitely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw() does not reserve liquid WRON for pending withdrawal requests

preconditions: Vault has insufficient liquid WRON to finalize the current epoch
Operator undelegates stake to provide liquidity

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: withdraw() allows pulling up to IERC20(asset()).balanceOf(address(this))
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Liquid WRON intended for epoch finalization is unprotected
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Opportunistic users bypass the withdrawal queue and lock up queued users' funds

economic_questions: Can queued users be permanently starved of liquidity?
Does this force the operator to continuously undelegate, losing staking yield?
validation_requirements: Simulate undelegation followed by an immediate liquid withdrawal
Verify that finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch reverts due to insufficient WRON

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Time-value of money (immediate exit vs queued exit)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: High withdrawal demand exceeding liquid reserves

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users waiting in the withdrawal queue

threats.title: Permanent Protocol DoS via Staking Contract Revert on Invalid Validator
threats.hypothesis_description: `totalAssets()` iterates over all known validators to calculate the total staked amount via `IRoninValidator.getManyStakingAmounts`. If the Ronin staking contract reverts when queried for a slashed or invalid validator, `totalAssets()` will consistently revert. This completely bricks the protocol, disabling deposits, withdrawals, and epoch finalizations. Furthermore, the recovery function `pruneValidatorList()` also calls `getManyStakingAmounts` and will similarly revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: Need to verify Ronin staking contract behavior on invalid/slashed validators to confirm if a revert is possible.
deployment_assumptions: Ronin staking contract reverts on invalid/slashed validator queries
threats.config_evidence: LiquidRon_saigon.s.sol uses real Ronin staking address 0x9C245671791834daf3885533D24dce516B763B28

proof_of_signal.pattern: Calls getManyStakingAmounts without try-catch

preconditions: A validator in the validators list becomes invalid or slashed
IRoninValidator.getManyStakingAmounts reverts for this validator

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: External call failure propagates to totalAssets()
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: External state change (slashing) breaks core vault accounting
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol becomes unusable, locking all funds

economic_questions: Does the Ronin staking contract actually revert for inactive validators?
Is there any fallback mechanism to bypass totalAssets?
validation_requirements: Test getManyStakingAmounts against a known slashed validator on Ronin
Verify if pruneValidatorList can succeed if getManyStakingAmounts fails

threats.title: delegateAmount Can Deploy Operator-Fee-Reserved WRON to Staking, Breaking Fee Withdrawal
threats.hypothesis_description: delegateAmount() calls _withdrawRONTo(proxy, total) which unwraps WRON from the vault balance and sends native RON to the proxy for delegation. There is no check ensuring that vault WRON balance remains >= operatorFeeAmount after delegation. If the operator delegates too much, the WRON backing accumulated operator fees is sent to validators. fetchOperatorFee() will then revert (insufficient WRON balance), and the fee obligation persists, permanently inflating totalAssets() until new deposits or undelegation occur.
threats.investigation_rationale: The vault WRON balance is shared between user deposits and accumulated operator fees, with no segregation or minimum reserve check. The operator can unknowingly or intentionally delegate fee-reserved WRON, creating an insolvency condition for fee withdrawals.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _withdrawRONTo(stakingProxy, total) sends full requested amount without checking vault_wron_balance >= operatorFeeAmount + total

preconditions: operatorFeeAmount > 0 (harvest has been called)
Operator delegates amount such that remaining vault WRON < operatorFeeAmount
No concurrent deposits to replenish vault WRON

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _withdrawRONTo(stakingProxy, total) has no guard: vault_wron_balance - total >= operatorFeeAmount
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Operator delegates all vault WRON leaving nothing for fee withdrawal
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Fee recipient cannot claim fees; totalAssets permanently inflated by operatorFeeAmount

economic_questions: What is the typical vault WRON liquidity vs total delegated amount?
Can this condition occur during normal operations without malice?
What is the cascading effect on share price if fee claims are blocked?
validation_requirements: Test delegateAmount with total > vault_wron_balance - operatorFeeAmount
Verify fetchOperatorFee reverts when WRON is insufficient
Model totalAssets behavior when operatorFeeAmount is permanently stuck

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: permanent totalAssets inflation benefits withdrawers
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Not directly profitable; enables ECON-H001 indefinitely

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Blocks fee extraction, preserves inflated share price

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Fee recipient (cannot withdraw), all LRON holders (inflated price distortion)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Vault WRON balance vs operatorFeeAmount after typical harvest
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm no reserve check exists in delegateAmount

threats.title: Unbounded Validator List Growth via _tryPushValidator Causes totalAssets DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: Every call to delegateAmount and redelegateAmount invokes _tryPushValidator for destination validators, adding new entries to the validators array. The array is only shrinkable via pruneValidatorList() which requires validators to have zero stake AND zero rewards across all proxies. Since totalAssets() iterates all validators for every proxy (O(validators * proxies) external calls to Ronin staking), unbounded growth can cause totalAssets() to exceed gas limits. This would DoS all deposit, withdraw, redeem, and requestWithdrawal operations since they all depend on totalAssets().
threats.investigation_rationale: delegateAmount and redelegateAmount add validators but never remove source validators. Pruning requires explicit external call and may itself fail with large lists. The deployment script already delegates to 5 validators across 2 proxies, establishing the pattern.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _tryPushValidator unconditionally adds to validators array with no upper bound; validators.push(_validator) grows array indefinitely

preconditions: Operator adds many distinct validator addresses over time
Pruning is not performed regularly or becomes too expensive
Multiple proxies amplify gas cost per validator

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalAssets cost ~= validators.length * proxies * external_call_gas. With 200 validators and 10 proxies, ~2000 external calls per totalAssets invocation
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Gradual validator accumulation or operator adding many validators in redelegation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol-wide DoS: all user operations blocked when totalAssets exceeds block gas limit

economic_questions: What is the gas cost per validator per proxy in getTotalStaked/getTotalRewards?
At what validator count does totalAssets exceed block gas limit?
Can pruneValidatorList itself be DoS'd at high validator counts?
validation_requirements: Benchmark gas cost of totalAssets with increasing validator counts
Determine Ronin chain block gas limit
Test pruneValidatorList gas consumption at scale

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; protocol DoS as griefing

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Slow accumulation; trigger point when gas limit exceeded
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete protocol freeze for all users

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Cannot deposit, withdraw, or redeem; funds locked

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current validator count (5 in deployment), proxy count (2), Ronin gas limit
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Gas profiling of totalAssets at various validator counts

threats.title: LiquidRon _withdraw Override Enables Vault-to-Self WRON Transfer Bypass During Withdrawal
threats.hypothesis_description: The _withdraw override in LiquidRon removes event emission and directly calls SafeERC20.safeTransfer to the receiver. When called from withdraw() or redeem(), the receiver is address(this) (the vault itself), making the WRON transfer a no-op (vault to vault). However, the subsequent _withdrawRONTo unwraps WRON and sends native RON to the actual receiver. If the vault's WRON balance is manipulable between the safeTransfer and _withdrawRONTo (e.g., via reentrancy through the receiver's callback), the unwrapped amount could differ from what was notionally transferred. More critically, the _withdraw function is also called by finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch with receiver=escrow, where the transfer IS meaningful. The absence of the Withdraw event in _withdraw means standard ERC4626 monitoring tools won't detect these internal transfers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetric behavior of _withdraw (no-op when receiver=self during user withdrawals, real transfer when receiver=escrow during finalization) creates different trust profiles for the same function, and the removed event impacts monitoring integrity.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _withdraw override removes Withdraw event emission and does SafeERC20.safeTransfer(asset(), receiver, assets) where receiver can be address(this) or escrow depending on caller path

preconditions: User calls withdraw() or redeem() (receiver=address(this), transfer is no-op)
Or operator calls finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch (receiver=escrow, real transfer)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: safeTransfer(vault, vault, assets) is a no-op but _withdrawRONTo(receiver, assets) still unwraps full amount
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If vault WRON balance < assets after no-op transfer but before unwrap (impossible in single tx, but monitoring blind spot)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Primarily a monitoring/detection blind spot; no direct profit but enables undetected state changes

economic_questions: Can the no-op self-transfer be exploited via reentrancy in the _withdrawRONTo callback?
Does removed Withdraw event affect any on-chain governance or monitoring contracts?
Is the asymmetric _withdraw behavior between user paths and finalization paths consistent?
validation_requirements: Trace full state changes through both _withdraw paths
Test reentrancy during _withdrawRONTo callback after _withdraw
Verify event emission correctness in outer withdraw/redeem functions

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Monitoring blind spot; indirect financial impact

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify no state inconsistency between paths

threats.title: Public pause/unpause can be rendered unusable due to Ownable inheritance ambiguity (dual-Ownable base) causing loss of emergency controls
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidRon inherits Ownable directly and also inherits Pausable, which itself inherits Ownable. This creates a multi-inheritance diamond with two Ownable constructors and potentially two owner storage layouts depending on compiler linearization and OZ version. If miscompiled or if inheritance order/constructor chaining behaves unexpectedly, pause/unpause and onlyOwner checks could reference an unexpected owner slot or revert, breaking emergency stop and admin control.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical operational risk: emergency pause is a core incident-response tool. Dual-Ownable inheritance can lead to deployment-time surprises, especially across OZ versions and if constructors are not consistently invoked.
deployment_assumptions: Compilation succeeds with the given inheritance graph and does not enforce single Ownable base
OnlyOwner modifiers across inherited paths refer to a consistent owner storage slot
threats.config_evidence: No explicit resolution of dual Ownable inheritance shown in LiquidRon.sol inheritance list; verify compiled linearization and storage layout in build artifacts (out/).

proof_of_signal.pattern: contract LiquidRon is ERC4626, RonHelper, Pausable, ValidatorTracker (LiquidRon also calls Ownable(msg.sender) in constructor while Pausable inherits Ownable)

preconditions: Project compiles with two Ownable inheritance paths without explicit override/virtual base resolution
Owner storage or constructor initialization is inconsistent across bases

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Emergency controls (pause/unpause) rely on onlyOwner; dual Ownable paths can cause onlyOwner to check the wrong owner slot in some inheritance layouts.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time an emergency pause is required; manifests as inability to pause/unpause or unexpected owner.

operational_questions: Does compiled bytecode include one or two Ownable storage variables / constructors?
Which owner does onlyOwner resolve to in LiquidRon and in Pausable at runtime?
validation_requirements: Inspect compiler linearization and storage layout (forge inspect LiquidRon storage)
Deploy to a testnet and verify owner(), pause(), unpause(), and onlyOwner setters behave consistently

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker does not need to act; risk manifests as operational failure.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: During an incident, protocol cannot pause/unpause or admin cannot operate.

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Possible accidental loss of admin authority / wrong owner reference.
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: High: inability to pause can amplify exploit impact; inability to unpause can freeze protocol.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All onlyOwner operations and incident response.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unexpected owner() value; onlyOwner reverts for expected owner.

threats.title: Escrow unlimited approval + lack of escape hatch can permanently strand WRON if vault deposit path breaks
threats.hypothesis_description: Escrow approves the vault for max WRON and has no method to recover tokens or update vault address. If LiquidRon.deposit(uint256,address) becomes unusable (paused indefinitely, owner lost, or unexpected revert due to ERC4626 changes), then WRON transferred into Escrow via LiquidRon.deposit() / receive() cannot be recovered, effectively locking user deposits routed through Escrow.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a hard lock risk caused by missing operational tooling (no rescue). It’s not theoretical: pausing is implemented, and integration breakage is a common production failure mode.
deployment_assumptions: Users primarily deposit via native deposit()/receive() route that moves WRON through Escrow
There is no off-chain operational procedure to avoid Escrow (e.g., only using ERC4626 deposit directly)
threats.config_evidence: Escrow has only deposit() and constructor approval; no withdraw/recover/update methods in Escrow.sol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Escrow approves vault max in constructor; no token rescue/withdraw; vault address immutable (_vault = msg.sender)

preconditions: WRON ends up in Escrow (via LiquidRon.deposit() or LiquidRon.receive())
Escrow.deposit cannot successfully call into LiquidRon.deposit(uint256,address) (e.g., due to paused state or unexpected revert)
No privileged rescue path exists

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Escrow is a custody hop but is not recoverable if the hop breaks; it assumes the vault deposit call always works.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner can pause LiquidRon, which could indirectly block Escrow->vault deposits.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any pause/incident window; could result in stuck funds.

operational_questions: Does pausing LiquidRon block Escrow.deposit callback path (it calls LiquidRon.deposit(uint256,address) which is whenNotPaused)?
Are there any off-chain safeguards to prevent users from depositing during pause?
validation_requirements: Test: pause vault, then send RON to LiquidRon.receive() and see if WRON becomes stuck in Escrow due to revert on Escrow.deposit()
Confirm whether receive()/deposit() route can partially succeed before revert (wrap+transfer already happened)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for protocol pause (or cause it indirectly via incident response).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Trigger user deposits through receive()/deposit() that move WRON to Escrow then revert on callback to vault, leaving funds stranded.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Low; could happen accidentally or be used for disruption by spamming deposits during pause.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: User deposits can become stuck and unrecoverable; support burden and reputational damage.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: WRON accumulating in Escrow without corresponding share mint events; reverted Escrow.deposit calls.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users reporting deposits not credited while escrow balance increases.

threats.title: Owner Can Manipulate operatorFee Before Epoch Finalization to Distort Locked Share Price
threats.hypothesis_description: The owner can call setOperatorFee() to change the fee without timelock, directly affecting getTotalRewards() and thus totalAssets(). By setting operatorFee to 0 before calling finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch(), the owner inflates totalAssets (since getTotalRewards returns gross rewards with no fee deduction). This locks a higher-than-correct price per share for the epoch. The owner then restores the fee. Withdrawal requesters for that epoch receive more assets than they should, at the expense of remaining depositors.
threats.investigation_rationale: The documentation acknowledges that changing operatorFee impacts totalAssets and states this is accepted behavior. However, the ability to atomically change fee → finalize → restore fee creates a specific exploit path where the owner extracts value from depositors into withdrawal requesters (potentially themselves).

proof_of_signal.pattern: setOperatorFee has no timelock; getTotalRewards at line 234 uses live operatorFee; finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch at line 216 calls previewRedeem which depends on totalAssets → getTotalRewards

preconditions: Owner/admin collusion or compromised owner key
Pending withdrawal requests exist for current epoch
Unclaimed staking rewards exist (affected by fee)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: getTotalRewards deducts (totalRewards * operatorFee / BIPS). With fee=0: deduction=0, totalAssets inflated by full fee portion. With fee=999: deduction = ~10% of rewards.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner sets fee to 0 → totalAssets increases by (rewards * currentFee / BIPS) → finalize locks inflated price → owner restores fee
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Withdrawal requesters receive (rewards * currentFee / BIPS) extra assets, taken from remaining depositors

economic_questions: Is the owner expected to be a multisig or EOA?
Can setOperatorFee and finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch be batched atomically?
What is the maximum inflation from fee manipulation?
validation_requirements: Calculate totalAssets delta when fee changes from 250 to 0
Simulate: setFee(0) → finalize → setFee(250) → measure impact on remaining depositors
Assess if documentation acceptance covers this specific attack path

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 2.5% of unclaimed rewards redirected to withdrawal requesters
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Direct extraction if owner is also a withdrawal requester

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Remaining depositors lose share value proportional to fee manipulation

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional share of fee manipulation amount

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: totalAssets comparison at fee=0 vs fee=250

threats.title: Unbounded Proxy Deployment Leads to Gas Exhaustion
threats.hypothesis_description: The owner can deploy an unlimited number of `LiquidProxy` contracts. Since functions like `getTotalStaked()` and `getTotalRewards()` iterate over all deployed proxies, deploying too many proxies will cause these view functions to exceed the block gas limit. This would permanently brick the `totalAssets()` calculation, disabling all core protocol functions and locking user funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: While owner-driven, this represents a permanent unrecoverable state if triggered accidentally or maliciously.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Pushes to stakingProxies without upper bound

preconditions: Owner deploys a large number of staking proxies

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: O(P * V) iteration where P is proxy count and V is validator count
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol becomes unusable, locking all funds

economic_questions: How many proxies are required to hit the gas limit?
Is there any way to remove or disable a proxy?
validation_requirements: Measure gas consumed per proxy in totalAssets
Verify absence of proxy removal functionality

threats.title: Integration Failure and Fund Loss due to Misnamed Mint Parameter
threats.hypothesis_description: The `mint` function overrides the standard ERC4626 `mint(uint256 shares, address receiver)` but names the first parameter `_assets`. If a frontend or external protocol integrates with this vault and passes an asset amount expecting it to mint that exact amount of assets, it will instead mint that amount of SHARES. This could result in pulling significantly more or less WRON from the user than intended.
threats.investigation_rationale: Parameter naming mismatch with standard ERC4626 interface creates severe integration risks for external protocols.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Parameter is _assets but passed to super.mint which expects shares

preconditions: External integration relies on parameter names from the ABI

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User accidentally spends more WRON than intended due to UI/integration error

economic_questions: How much excess WRON could be pulled if share price > 1?
Are there standard frontends (e.g., Zapper, Yearn) that rely on parameter names?
validation_requirements: Check how popular web3 libraries parse and display this ABI
Calculate financial impact of a 10x share price difference

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: User must have high WRON allowance
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When share price deviates significantly from 1:1

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: User's WRON allowance to the vault

threats.title: No Withdrawal Request Cancellation Creates Irreversible Fund Lock If Finalization Blocked
threats.hypothesis_description: When users call requestWithdrawal(), their LRON shares are transferred to the vault contract and locked in the current epoch. There is no cancel function to return shares to the user. If finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() cannot be called (due to the onlyOperator bug per H001, pause state, insufficient vault WRON, or operator unavailability), all shares locked in the current epoch are permanently stuck. Users lose their shares with no recourse. Combined with INV-ACCESS-001, if the owner accidentally sets operator[owner]=true, both operator functions AND finalization are locked, making ALL pending withdrawal requests permanently irrecoverable.
threats.investigation_rationale: requestWithdrawal at line 282 transfers shares to vault. No function exists to cancel/return shares from a non-finalized epoch. The shares can only be recovered through finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch + redeem flow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: requestWithdrawal transfers shares to vault, increments lockedSharesPerEpoch, but no cancelWithdrawal function exists anywhere in the contract

preconditions: Users have called requestWithdrawal for the current epoch
finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch cannot be called (operator bug, pause, or insufficient funds)
No code path exists to return shares from a non-finalized epoch

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Shares transferred to vault with no return path if finalization fails
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Entirely dependent on operator/owner being able to call finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any period where finalization is blocked

operational_questions: Should a cancelWithdrawal function exist for non-finalized epochs?
What is the expected SLA for epoch finalization?
How much user value could be locked if finalization is delayed?
validation_requirements: Confirm no alternative path to recover shares from non-finalized epoch
Test scenario: request withdrawal, then lock out operator functions, verify shares are stuck
Assess if owner can transfer locked shares directly (owner has no such function)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Users normally call requestWithdrawal expecting timely finalization
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If operator functions become inaccessible (H001 or pause), finalization stops
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User shares are locked in vault with no cancellation mechanism
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Funds effectively lost until code fix/redeployment
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — systemic risk from missing functionality

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All withdrawal requests for current epoch permanently locked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code upgrade or manual intervention — no on-chain recovery path

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: withdrawalEpoch not incrementing for extended period
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: User complaints about stuck withdrawal requests

threats.title: Owner Setting operator[owner]=true Locks All Operator Functions Including Self
threats.hypothesis_description: Due to the inverted onlyOperator modifier logic (H001), if the owner calls updateOperator(owner, true) — a natural action to enable themselves as an operator — the modifier condition becomes: `msg.sender != owner()` is false, `operator[msg.sender]` is true, so `false || true = true` → reverts. The owner is now locked out of ALL operator functions (harvest, delegate, undelegate, redelegate, finalize). Since the owner is the ONLY entity that could previously pass the modifier, all staking operations cease entirely. Recovery requires calling updateOperator(owner, false), but this requires the owner to diagnose the issue. During the lockout window, rewards are not harvested, delegations cannot be managed, and withdrawal epochs cannot be finalized.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a specific catastrophic consequence of H001. The documentation states operators and owner should both have access. An owner self-flagging is a highly plausible action that creates a complete protocol lockout.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onlyOperator: if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert — owner with operator[owner]=true causes (false || true) = true → revert

preconditions: Owner calls updateOperator(owner, true)
Owner then attempts any onlyOperator function

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Self-flagging as operator is a natural administrative action that creates a complete lockout
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner action directly causes the lockout; recovery also requires owner action
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From the moment operator[owner]=true until owner diagnoses and reverses

operational_questions: Is there any operational procedure that could lead the owner to flag themselves as operator?
How quickly can the owner diagnose and recover from this lockout?
What is the maximum damage during the lockout window?
validation_requirements: Set operator[owner]=true, attempt any operator function — verify revert
Verify updateOperator(owner, false) restores access
Measure impact on pending withdrawal requests during lockout

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner naturally calls updateOperator(owner, true) to enable self as operator
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All operator functions immediately start reverting for owner
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Staking operations, harvesting, and withdrawal finalization cease
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol frozen until owner realizes issue and calls updateOperator(owner, false)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Owner must self-diagnose; no external party can resolve

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete loss of staking management capability
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total protocol freeze — no harvesting, delegation, or withdrawal finalization
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low if diagnosed quickly — updateOperator(owner, false) restores access

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: updateOperator(owner, true) transaction followed by failed operator function calls
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: All operator functions reverting after operator role update
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Owner repeatedly attempting and failing operator calls

threats.title: Phantom operator fee accrual via harvestAndDelegateRewards enables systematic depositor principal drain when fees are fetched
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidRon.harvestAndDelegateRewards credits operatorFeeAmount based on the value returned from proxy.harvestAndDelegateRewards, but in that path rewards are re-delegated rather than transferred into the vault as WRON. This creates a growing fee liability not backed by vault assets; when fetchOperatorFee is called, WRON is withdrawn from the vault balance (user principal) to pay the feeRecipient.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code-level solvency/accounting bug with direct value transfer. Realism is LOW only because calling harvestAndDelegateRewards and fetchOperatorFee is privileged (owner due to onlyOperator bug, or operator if fixed). It is still critical to validate because it can occur during routine operations.

proof_of_signal.lines: harvestAndDelegateRewards(): operatorFeeAmount += ...; fetchOperatorFee(): withdraws WRON from vault to feeRecipient
proof_of_signal.pattern: Fee accrual without corresponding asset inflow to vault

preconditions: A privileged caller executes harvestAndDelegateRewards at least once
operatorFee > 0
feeRecipient executes fetchOperatorFee before sufficient liquid rewards are realized into the vault

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Fee accrues during auto-compounding even though no WRON enters the vault from that action
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time compounding is used instead of harvesting-to-vault

operational_questions: Does IRoninValidator.delegateRewards return an amount that represents actual rewards moved (and is it ever transferred to the proxy/vault)?
How frequently is harvestAndDelegateRewards expected to run operationally?
Is operatorFeeAmount intended to be paid from principal or from realized rewards only?
validation_requirements: Fork test: call harvestAndDelegateRewards and verify vault WRON balance does not increase while operatorFeeAmount does
Simulate subsequent fetchOperatorFee and quantify depositor principal reduction
Determine if accounting can be corrected by realizing rewards before fee withdrawal

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Privileged actor repeatedly calls harvestAndDelegateRewards to maximize operatorFeeAmount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Fee recipient calls fetchOperatorFee to withdraw fees from vault WRON
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Depositors experience loss of backing / reduced redeem value
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat to drain significant TVL over time
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Privileged caller and feeRecipient (may be same entity)

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Partial (feeRecipient can extract depositor principal indirectly)
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Solvency erosion; inability for users to withdraw if liquid WRON depleted

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low (single admin can do both if feeRecipient controlled)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: operatorFeeAmount increasing without corresponding WRON inflows; repeated harvestAndDelegateRewards
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Fee withdrawals exceeding realized rewards; vault WRON balance shrinking during compounding periods
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Fee recipient withdraws shortly after compounding calls

threats.title: Broken native deposit flow due to payable mismatch: Escrow.deposit calls IVault.deposit payable but LiquidRon.deposit(uint256,address) is nonpayable
threats.hypothesis_description: Escrow.deposit calls IVault(payable(_vault)).deposit(_amount, _receiver) through an interface that marks the function as payable. However, LiquidRon.deposit(uint256,address) (the actual ERC4626 deposit override) is not payable. This can cause the native deposit() and receive() flows that route through Escrow to revert (depending on compiler/interface call semantics and whether any value is forwarded). If it reverts, users cannot deposit native RON as intended.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a hard operational failure mode (deposit path dead) that can be missed in review because interface mismatch compiles. It must be validated with an end-to-end test on the target chain.
deployment_assumptions: Escrow.deposit is called without value but still fails due to calling a nonpayable function via payable interface, or some value is forwarded unintentionally in the call chain
threats.config_evidence: Deployment script uses liquidRon.deposit{value: 10000 ether}(); implying native deposit is expected to work, but does not prove it succeeded on-chain.

proof_of_signal.lines: interface IVault { function deposit(uint256 _amount, address _receiver) external payable; } then IVault(payable(_vault)).deposit(_amount, _receiver);
proof_of_signal.pattern: Payable interface call into nonpayable implementation (LiquidRon.deposit(uint256,address))

preconditions: User uses LiquidRon.deposit() (payable) or sends native RON to LiquidRon.receive()
Escrow.deposit forwards into LiquidRon.deposit(uint256,address) and triggers nonpayable rejection or other revert condition

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Native RON deposits revert while ERC20 WRON deposits work
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time users try native deposits

operational_questions: Does Escrow.deposit forward msg.value (should be 0) and does the EVM enforce payable at runtime based on callee mutability?
Does native deposit path succeed on a forked Ronin environment with the actual WRON contract?
Are there chain-specific quirks for WRON deposit/transfer that impact this path?
validation_requirements: End-to-end test: call LiquidRon.deposit{value:X} and confirm shares minted and WRON moved as expected
Test receive() native transfer path
If failure occurs, identify exact revert reason and fix interface/implementation mismatch

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Transaction reverts, preventing deposits and growth of TVL
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Protocol becomes unusable for native RON deposit UX (key product feature)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Native deposit feature unavailable; reduced adoption; support burden
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium (requires redeploy/upgrade; no upgrade pattern exists)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted native deposits; absence of Deposit events for native deposit attempts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Escrow WRON balance increasing without corresponding share mints (if partial progress occurs before revert)
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users resorting to ERC20 WRON deposit only

threats.title: fetchOperatorFee Bypasses Pause Allowing Fee Extraction During Emergency
threats.hypothesis_description: fetchOperatorFee() lacks the whenNotPaused modifier, while all user operations (deposit, withdraw, redeem, requestWithdrawal) are paused. During a pause triggered for an emergency (e.g., discovering the harvestAndDelegateRewards phantom fee bug), the fee recipient can still extract operatorFeeAmount from the vault WRON balance. Combined with the phantom fee issue (INV-STATE-001), the fee recipient could drain user deposits via phantom operatorFeeAmount while users are blocked from withdrawing.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetry between user operations (paused) and fee operations (unpaused) creates a window where the fee recipient has exclusive extraction ability. If operatorFeeAmount includes phantom fees from harvestAndDelegateRewards, this extraction drains user principal.

proof_of_signal.pattern: fetchOperatorFee() has no whenNotPaused modifier, unlike all user-facing functions which are gated by whenNotPaused

preconditions: Protocol is paused by owner
operatorFeeAmount > 0
Fee recipient is colluding or compromised

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: fetchOperatorFee withdraws operatorFeeAmount with no pause check while users cannot withdraw
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Pause → extract fees → unpause with reduced vault balance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Fee recipient extracts full operatorFeeAmount (including phantom fees from harvestAndDelegateRewards) while users are locked out

economic_questions: What is the maximum operatorFeeAmount at any given time?
Can phantom fees from harvestAndDelegateRewards compound to significant amounts?
Is there any circuit breaker on fee withdrawal size?
validation_requirements: Confirm fetchOperatorFee is callable while paused
Calculate maximum drainable amount via phantom fee accumulation
Test pause → fetchOperatorFee → unpause scenario

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 100% of extracted amount (no cost beyond gas)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Vault WRON reduced, all users affected on unpause

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Share of drained WRON distributed across all holders

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Accumulated operatorFeeAmount, frequency of harvestAndDelegateRewards usage
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify callable during pause, verify phantom fee accumulation

threats.title: updateFeeRecipient to Non-RON-Receivable Address Permanently Blocks Fee Withdrawal, Inflating Share Price
threats.hypothesis_description: updateFeeRecipient() accepts any address without validating it can receive native RON. If set to a contract that rejects native RON (no receive/fallback), fetchOperatorFee() will always revert in _withdrawRONTo. operatorFeeAmount cannot be withdrawn and grows with each harvest. Since totalAssets() includes operatorFeeAmount in the vault WRON balance, totalAssets is permanently inflated, distorting the share price. New depositors receive fewer shares than fair value, while withdrawers extract inflated value indefinitely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The feeRecipient can be changed by the owner at any time to any address. There is no validation that the address can accept native RON, which is required by _withdrawRONTo. A malicious or negligent owner could set this to a non-receivable address, creating a permanent accounting distortion.
threats.config_evidence: LiquidRon_saigon.s.sol: feeRecipient = 0x00000000000CbA43Ae9379E73c0ccB4E08D7eD30 — needs verification this address can receive native RON

proof_of_signal.pattern: updateFeeRecipient sets feeRecipient = _feeRecipient with no validation; fetchOperatorFee uses _withdrawRONTo which sends native RON via call{value}

preconditions: Owner sets feeRecipient to address that cannot receive native RON
operatorFeeAmount > 0

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No _checkUserCanReceiveRon or similar validation on feeRecipient
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Set feeRecipient to contract without receive() → fetchOperatorFee permanently reverts → operatorFeeAmount inflates totalAssets indefinitely
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Chronic share price inflation benefiting withdrawers at expense of new depositors

economic_questions: What is the long-term effect of permanently stuck operatorFeeAmount on share price?
Can the owner fix this by calling updateFeeRecipient again?
What if the initial feeRecipient becomes non-receivable (e.g., self-destructs)?
validation_requirements: Test updateFeeRecipient with non-receivable address
Verify fetchOperatorFee reverts
Confirm owner can recover by updating feeRecipient

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: New depositors receiving fewer shares than fair value
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to operatorFeeAmount relative to totalAssets

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: feeRecipient address capabilities, operatorFeeAmount growth rate
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify revert behavior and owner recovery path
